notes.text: Thomas Anderson lives a double life: by day, a mild-mannered programmer at Metacortex; by night, a hacker called Neo who sells illegal code and stares at screens looking for something he cannot name. The ordinary world is a cage he already senses — he just doesn't know it's a cage.

Neo is introduced as a man hollowed out by a question he cannot articulate. He is intelligent, isolated, and vaguely dissatisfied — exactly the profile of someone ready to be called. His ordinary world is already extraordinary by real-world standards; the film's point is that it still isn't enough.
The city at the beginning of the film: grey, rainy, corporate. It looks like a real city — and that is the point. The ordinary world must be convincing enough that we accept it before we question it.

The ordinary world establishes the film's central metaphor before it is named: the comfortable lie. Neo's life is not bad — it is hollow. The film argues that the absence of truth is its own kind of suffering, even when the suffering is invisible.

A message appears on Neo's screen: 'Follow the white rabbit.' He follows it to a club where Trinity — a legendary hacker he has heard of — tells him the Matrix has him and that Morpheus can show him what it is. 'You've been living in a dream world, Neo.'

Trinity is the call's human face — she is not just a messenger but the proof that the call is real. A woman Neo respects has crossed the threshold and come back to find him. Her presence makes the invitation credible and the stakes personal.

The call is not a dramatic event but a quiet insistence: something is wrong, and Neo already knows it. The white rabbit motif (Alice in Wonderland) signals that what is being offered is not escape from reality but entry into a deeper one.

Neo is captured by Agent Smith before he can meet Morpheus. Offered a choice — be freed or be deleted — he refuses to cooperate. He is released back into his ordinary life with a tracking device removed from his body. He did not fully refuse the call; he simply failed to answer it in time.

Agent Smith's first appearance is the refusal's hidden engine — Neo does not refuse out of cowardice but out of confusion. He cannot yet see what he is being called toward. The agents represent the system's power to punish curiosity.

The refusal phase is unusually brief because Neo's resistance is intellectual, not moral. He does not refuse to know the truth — he simply cannot yet believe it is available. The ordinary world's most powerful tool is not violence but plausibility.

Morpheus contacts Neo and guides him by phone through an escape from agents. They meet. Morpheus offers two pills: blue to forget, red to see how deep the rabbit hole goes. 'I'm trying to free your mind, Neo. But I can only show you the door.'

Morpheus is the archetypal mentor — certain, weathered, and deliberately incomplete. He gives Neo tools and faith but withholds proof, because the point is not to be convinced but to choose. His belief in Neo is total and costs him everything by the film's end.

The mentor's gift is not the red pill — it is the question. Morpheus does not free Neo; he offers him the chance to free himself. The distinction matters: freedom imposed is just a different kind of prison.

Neo takes the red pill and is pulled from the liquid pod where his real body has always been — pale, atrophied, seeing the actual world for the first time. The Matrix is a simulation. He was never where he thought he was.

The threshold crossing is a physical rebirth — Neo emerges from the pod the way an infant emerges into the world, blind, helpless, and screaming. He must learn to be a body before he can learn to be the One.

The real world: grey, ruined, cold. The Nebuchadnezzar is a salvaged hovercraft crewed by people who look like survivors of something terrible — because they are. The contrast with the Matrix's constructed comfort is immediate and total.

The threshold crossing answers the ordinary world's question: the comfortable lie is revealed. But the film immediately poses a harder question — knowing the truth is worse than not knowing it, at least in the short term. Is the truth worth the cost?

Neo trains in simulated combat programs, studies the Matrix's rules, and meets the crew. Cypher reveals his resentment of the truth early — a betrayal seed planted casually. In the Matrix, Neo meets the Oracle, who tells him he is not the One.

Tank is the crew's heart — born free, never jacked in, devoted to the mission without any illusion about what was sacrificed. His loyalty to Morpheus and belief in Neo are the film's moral centre without any of the mysticism.

Cypher is the test phase's shadow: a man who chose the truth and wants to undo the choice. His betrayal is not villainy but exhaustion — he cannot live with knowing what he knows. He is Neo's nightmare future, the road not taken made flesh.

The Oracle tells Neo he is not the One — but also that he has the gift and is waiting for something, maybe his next life. Her prophecy is deliberately ambiguous: she is not predicting the future but creating the conditions for Neo to choose it.
Morpheus tells the crew they are going back in to visit the Oracle again — a routine trip that Cypher has already betrayed to the agents. The crew enters the Matrix. The trap is already set.

Neo's approach to the inmost cave is shadowed by the Oracle's verdict. He believes he is not the One — which means Morpheus is wrong, and Morpheus has staked everything on being right. The psychological weight of this is heavier than any agent.

The approach phase closes around the crew like a net. The danger is not yet visible — that is what makes it dread rather than fear. Every casual moment in this phase carries the weight of what is coming.

Cypher kills Switch and Apoc inside the Matrix. Tank survives and kills Cypher before he can unplug Neo. Agents capture Morpheus. Neo and Trinity escape — but Morpheus is being interrogated for the Zion access codes, and time is running out.

Morpheus's capture is the ordeal's sacrifice — the mentor is taken so the hero must act without guidance. Neo must decide what kind of person he is without Morpheus there to tell him who he can become.

The ordeal forces Neo's first genuine choice: leave Morpheus (the logical, survivable option) or go back in (irrational, almost certainly fatal). He chooses to go back. This is the moment the One stops being a prophecy and becomes a person.

Neo and Trinity storm the government building, rescue Morpheus, and escape to the rooftop — where a helicopter awaits. Neo catches Trinity mid-fall after an impossible leap. He is beginning to bend the rules of the system without fully realising it.

The reward is not power but proof — Neo begins to trust what he is capable of. He stops managing the world by its rules and starts acting from instinct. The gap between what he thinks he can do and what he actually does is narrowing.

The reward beat delivers the film's first pure exhilaration — not because the danger is past (it isn't) but because Neo is beginning to inhabit his own potential. The Oracle's ambiguous prophecy starts to feel like it was pointing somewhere real.

Morpheus is free — but Agent Smith pursues Neo through the subway and kills him. Trinity's voice over the phone: 'The Oracle told me I would fall in love with the One.' Neo is dead on the platform. The road back has become a body.

Agent Smith, victorious over Neo's body, delivers a small monologue about the inevitability of death — and then empties an entire clip into Neo's body, just to be certain. It is the system's last confident act.

The road back is the moment of maximum cost: the hero is dead. The trilogy's question — can a person be more than the system that made them? — appears to have been answered in the negative. The machine has won.

Trinity tells Neo's body she loves him and that the Oracle told her she would love the One — therefore he cannot be dead. He stands. He sees the code beneath the Matrix. Agent Smith attacks and Neo stops his fist with one hand, then disassembles him from the inside.

The resurrection is triggered by love, not training — which is the film's most deliberate departure from action-movie logic. Neo does not level up; he is called back by something that cannot be quantified or programmed. The system has no response to this.

Trinity's love is not passive — it is causal. She does not wait; she declares. Her belief in Neo is the force that resolves the Oracle's ambiguous prophecy. 'He is the One' because she chose to treat him as such, and the world responded.
The resurrection answers the ordinary world's question with the same force: the truth is worth the cost, but only because the cost includes being loved by someone who sees your truth before you do.

Neo flies. He hangs in the air above the city — the Matrix's geography now as readable to him as code. An operator tells him 'There are so many of us.' The machines send more agents. Neo accelerates into the sky.

Neo's return with the elixir is not a cure he delivers to the world — it is the proof that the world can be changed. He cannot free everyone yet. But the possibility, once demonstrated, cannot be uncreated.

The elixir is the knowledge that the Matrix is not inevitable — that its rules can be broken by someone willing to see them as rules rather than facts. Neo cannot liberate humanity alone. But now they know liberation is possible, and that changes everything.

The final image — Neo ascending through the sky, Rage Against the Machine on the soundtrack — is one of cinema's most purely kinetic closing shots. It is not a resolution but a beginning: the monomyth's return opens into sequel, into the world, into the question of what comes next.
